en: help and advice about how to do something or about how to deal with problems
examples: Children need guidance from their parents.
The teacher provided guidance on how to write the essay.

en: cruel, severe, and unkind; unpleasant and difficult to live in
examples: The judge imposed a harsh sentence.
The harsh winter weather made travelling difficult.

en: the history, traditions, and qualities that a country or society has had for many years
examples: The city is proud of its cultural heritage.
These buildings are part of our national heritage.

en: a system in which people or things are arranged according to their importance
examples: There is a strict hierarchy within the organization.
He quickly rose through the corporate hierarchy.

en: concerned with reducing suffering and improving the conditions that people live in
examples: The organization provides humanitarian aid to refugees.
The country is facing a humanitarian crisis.

en: an idea or explanation of something that is based on a few known facts but that has not yet been proved to be true
examples: The scientist put forward a hypothesis to explain the results.
We need to test this hypothesis with further experiments.

en: a set of beliefs, especially one held by a particular group, that influences the way people behave
examples: Political ideology shapes government policy.
The two countries are divided by ideology.

en: a lack of knowledge or information about something
examples: His ignorance of the law is no excuse.
Public ignorance about the disease needs to be addressed.

en: to make the meaning of something clearer by using examples, pictures, etc.
examples: The report illustrates the need for more investment.
The book is beautifully illustrated with colour photographs.

en: extremely large or great
examples: The task requires an immense amount of patience.
She felt immense relief when the results were announced.

en: connected with an empire; connected with the system for measuring length, weight, etc. using pounds, inches, etc.
examples: The imperial palace was built in the 15th century.
Britain converted from the imperial system to the metric system.

en: to make something that has been officially decided start to happen or be used
examples: The government has failed to implement the reforms.
The new system will be implemented next year.

en: to introduce a new law, rule, tax, etc. and force people to accept it
examples: The government imposed a ban on smoking in public places.
I don't want to impose my views on anyone.

zh: 给……留下深刻印象；使钦佩
en: to make someone feel admiration and respect
examples: She dressed smartly to impress at the job interview.
His cooking skills really impressed the dinner guests.

en: a sudden strong desire to do something without thinking about the results
examples: I bought the jacket on impulse and regretted it later.
She had a sudden impulse to call her old friend.

en: the extent to which something happens or has an effect
examples: There is a high incidence of flu during winter months.
The incidence of food allergies has increased in recent years.

en: to tend to think or behave in a particular way
examples: I incline towards the vegetarian option when eating out.
He's inclined to stay home rather than go to parties.

en: to include something as part of a group, system, or plan
examples: The new menu incorporates dishes from several countries.
Try to incorporate more vegetables into your daily meals.

en: impossible or very difficult to believe; extremely good
examples: The view from the mountain top was absolutely incredible.
It's incredible how fast children grow up.

en: a sign that shows you what something is like or how a situation is changing
examples: Dark clouds are a good indicator of coming rain.
Your energy level is an indicator of your overall health.

en: belonging to a particular place rather than coming from somewhere else
examples: We visited a market selling indigenous crafts and foods.
Many indigenous plants in this region are used in traditional cooking.

en: to cause something to happen or to persuade someone to do something
examples: Too much coffee can induce anxiety and sleeplessness.
Nothing could induce me to go bungee jumping.

zh: 不可避免的；必然发生的
en: certain to happen and impossible to avoid or prevent
examples: Traffic jams are inevitable during the holiday season.
It was inevitable that the old shop would eventually close.

en: a baby or very young child
examples: The store has a special section for infant clothing.
Infants need to be fed every few hours.

en: to make someone or something suffer something unpleasant
examples: The storm inflicted serious damage on the coastal town.
I didn't mean to inflict so much stress on my family by moving abroad.

en: having a lot of influence on someone or something
examples: She follows several influential food bloggers on social media.
He became one of the most influential teachers at the school.

en: the basic systems and services that a country or organization needs in order to work properly
examples: The city invested heavily in transport infrastructure like roads and bridges.
Poor internet infrastructure makes working from home difficult in rural areas.

en: to live in a particular place
examples: Several rare bird species inhabit the wetlands near our village.
The island has been inhabited for thousands of years.

en: existing as a natural or basic part of something
examples: There is an inherent risk in any outdoor adventure sport.
Creativity is an inherent part of cooking without a recipe.

en: to receive money, property, or a title from someone who has died; to have qualities that a parent or relative has
examples: She inherited her grandmother's love of gardening.
He inherited the family house when his father passed away.

en: to put a drug or substance into the body using a needle; to add a quality to something
examples: The nurse injected the vaccine into my arm.
We need to inject some fun into this boring weekend.

en: a new idea, method, or invention
examples: Smartphone technology has been one of the biggest innovations of our time.
The restaurant is known for its innovation in plant-based cooking.

en: ideas, advice, effort, or information that is put into something
examples: We value your input on how to improve our service.
The project benefited from input from team members of all ages.

en: a request for information; an official investigation
examples: I made an inquiry about flight prices for the summer holiday.
The shop received several inquiries about the new product.

en: to look at something carefully to check that it is correct or in good condition
examples: Always inspect fruit carefully before buying it at the market.
The landlord came to inspect the flat before we moved out.

en: to give someone the desire, confidence, or enthusiasm to do something
examples: Her travel stories inspired me to book my own trip abroad.
The cooking show inspired him to try making pasta from scratch.

en: one of a number of payments that are made regularly over a period of time until something has been paid for
examples: We paid for the sofa in six monthly installments.
The final installment of the TV series airs next week.

en: a particular example or case of something
examples: There have been several instances of food poisoning at that restaurant.
In this instance, I think it's better to wait before making a decision.

en: a natural tendency to behave in a particular way without having to learn it or think about it
examples: My first instinct was to check if everyone was okay after the accident.
Trust your instincts when something doesn't feel right.

en: complete and not damaged
examples: Luckily, the package arrived with everything intact.
The old castle remains mostly intact after hundreds of years.

en: to combine two or more things so that they work together; to become part of a group
examples: It took a while to integrate into the new neighbourhood after moving.
The school tries to integrate technology into everyday learning.

en: the quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
examples: She is known for her integrity and always keeps her promises.
The integrity of the old building was tested by the earthquake.

en: connected with or using a person's ability to think in a logical way and understand things
examples: Reading is a great way to get intellectual stimulation.
They enjoyed intellectual conversations over coffee.

en: very great or extreme in strength or degree
examples: The intense heat during the summer made it hard to sleep.
She felt intense pressure before the final exam.

en: to communicate or react with each other
examples: Children learn social skills by interacting with other kids at school.
The app allows customers to interact directly with the shop owner.

en: to get involved in a situation that does not concern you; to prevent something from working properly
examples: Please don't interfere with my plans for the weekend.
The loud music from next door interfered with my studying.

en: the inside part of something, especially a building or vehicle
examples: The interior of the café was decorated in a cosy vintage style.
She hired a designer to redo the interior of her apartment.

en: between two stages, levels, or positions
examples: She signed up for an intermediate yoga class.
This textbook is designed for intermediate-level English learners.

en: to explain or decide the meaning of something; to translate spoken words from one language to another
examples: She interpreted his silence as disagreement.
A guide interpreted for us during our trip to Japan.

en: to become involved in a situation in order to improve or help it
examples: A neighbour intervened when they heard the argument getting louder.
The teacher had to intervene to stop the children from fighting.

en: having a close and personal relationship; private and personal
examples: They had an intimate dinner for two at a quiet restaurant.
She shared intimate details about her childhood with her best friend.

en: to enter a place in large numbers, especially in order to take control; to affect something in an unwanted way
examples: Ants invaded the kitchen after we left food on the counter.
Tourists invade the small coastal town every summer.

en: impossible to see
examples: The stain was almost invisible after she cleaned the shirt.
He felt invisible at the party because nobody talked to him.

en: the state of being separate from other people or things
examples: Working from home can sometimes lead to feelings of isolation.
The village's isolation made it difficult to get fresh supplies.

en: a written record of what you do each day; a newspaper or magazine that deals with a particular subject
examples: She keeps a journal to write down her thoughts before bed.
He published his research in an international science journal.

en: connected with a court of law, a judge, or a legal judgement
examples: The judicial system is supposed to treat everyone equally.
A judicial review was ordered to examine the decision.

en: very interested in or enthusiastic about something
examples: She's a keen gardener and spends every weekend in her garden.
He's keen to learn how to cook Italian food.

en: everything you can see when you look across a large area of land
examples: The train journey offered stunning views of the countryside landscape.
The landscape changes dramatically as you drive from the coast to the mountains.

en: recently; in the recent past
examples: I've been feeling really tired lately.
Have you seen any good films lately?

en: being the second of two things or people that have just been mentioned
examples: Between tea and coffee, I prefer the latter.
The latter half of the trip was much more enjoyable.

en: to start something new or make a new product available for the first time
examples: The company launched a new range of organic snacks.
She launched her own online clothing store last year.

en: to jump high or a long way; to increase suddenly by a large amount
examples: The cat leapt onto the kitchen counter to steal some fish.
House prices leapt by 10% in just one year.

en: allowed by law; fair or reasonable
examples: Make sure you buy from a legitimate online seller.
He had a legitimate reason for arriving late to work.

en: time when you are not working or studying and can relax and do things you enjoy
examples: I like to spend my leisure time reading or going for walks.
The hotel has a pool and other leisure facilities.

en: legally responsible for something; likely to do something
examples: The shop is not liable for items left in the changing room.
You're liable to catch a cold if you go out without a jacket.

zh: 开明的；慷慨的；自由主义的
en: willing to accept different opinions and ways of behaving; generous
examples: She has very liberal views on education and parenting.
He was liberal with his compliments at the dinner party.

en: in the same way; also
examples: "It was lovely meeting you." "Likewise!"
He grabbed a coffee, and I did likewise.

en: an arm or leg of a person or animal; a large branch of a tree
examples: He stretched his tired limbs after the long flight.
The storm broke several large limbs off the old oak tree.

en: going from one thing to another in a single direction or series of stages
examples: Career paths are rarely linear — most people change direction several times.
The story is told in a linear fashion, from beginning to end.

en: to stay somewhere for longer than expected; to continue to exist for longer than expected
examples: We lingered over coffee at the café, chatting for hours.
The smell of freshly baked bread lingered in the kitchen all morning.

en: the ability to read and write; knowledge of a particular subject
examples: The charity works to improve literacy rates among children.
Financial literacy is an important skill for managing your money.

en: in the most basic or original meaning of a word or expression; used to emphasize something
examples: The town was literally flooded — water covered all the streets.
I literally just walked through the door when the phone rang.

en: seeming natural, reasonable, or sensible
examples: It seemed logical to take the shorter route to avoid traffic.
There must be a logical explanation for why the door was open.

en: without any other people or things; single
examples: A lone jogger was running along the beach at sunrise.
She was the lone vegetarian at the barbecue.

en: continuing for a long period of time into the future
examples: We need a long-term plan for saving enough to buy a house.
Regular exercise has long-term benefits for your health.

en: the quality of being faithful in your support of someone or something
examples: Customer loyalty is rewarded with a 10% discount card.
His loyalty to his friends has never been questioned.

en: the great size or importance of something
examples: I didn't fully understand the magnitude of the problem until it was too late.
An earthquake of that magnitude can cause serious damage.

en: considered normal because it is shared by most people in a society
examples: Veganism has become much more mainstream in recent years.
The band started as an indie group but soon entered the mainstream music scene.

en: to show something clearly, especially a feeling or quality
examples: Her nervousness manifested itself as a slight trembling in her hands.
Stress can manifest in many different physical symptoms.

en: to control or influence someone or something in a clever or dishonest way
examples: He tried to manipulate his colleagues into doing his share of the work.
The software allows you to manipulate images easily.

zh: 边缘的；微小的；不重要的
en: small and not important; not part of the main group
examples: There was only a marginal improvement in sales this quarter.
The difference in taste between the two brands is marginal.

en: behaving in a sensible way, like an adult; fully grown or developed
examples: She's very mature for her age and handles problems calmly.
This cheese is left to mature for at least twelve months.

en: to increase something as much as possible
examples: Arrange the furniture carefully to maximize the space in a small flat.
She tried to maximize her holiday time by booking flights at the best times.

en: a set of moving parts in a machine; a method or system for achieving something
examples: The lock mechanism on the front door is broken.
Exercise is a great mechanism for dealing with stress.

en: connected with the Middle Ages (about AD 600 to AD 1500)
examples: We visited a beautiful medieval castle during our trip to Spain.
The town centre still has its medieval walls and narrow streets.

en: used to emphasize that something is small, unimportant, or not enough
examples: I'm not criticizing — I'm merely making a suggestion.
She's merely a beginner, so don't expect perfect results.

en: the quality of being good or worthy of praise; an advantage
examples: Each application will be judged on its own merits.
One of the merits of living in the countryside is the fresh air.

en: used to emphasize how small or unimportant something is
examples: The mere thought of public speaking makes her nervous.
He's a mere child — you can't expect him to understand.

en: to move from one place to another, especially to find work or better living conditions
examples: Many birds migrate south during the winter months.
His grandparents migrated to Australia in search of a better life.

en: not severe or strong; gentle
examples: We had a mild winter this year with very little snow.
The curry was too mild for my taste — I prefer spicy food.

en: to reduce something to the smallest possible level or amount
examples: Wearing sunscreen helps minimize the risk of sunburn.
We rearranged the schedule to minimize waiting time for customers.

en: a government department that has a particular area of responsibility
examples: The Ministry of Education announced changes to the school curriculum.
She works at the ministry dealing with environmental protection.

en: not very large, important, or serious
examples: It was only a minor injury — just a small cut on his finger.
There are a few minor changes I'd like to make to the travel plan.

en: the ability to move or travel easily from one place, job, or social class to another
examples: A good public transport system improves mobility for everyone.
Yoga helps improve your flexibility and mobility as you age.

en: average in amount, intensity, or degree; not extreme
examples: Doctors recommend moderate exercise, like a 30-minute daily walk.
The hotel was clean and comfortable at a moderate price.

en: to change something slightly, especially to improve it or make it less extreme
examples: I modified the recipe to make it dairy-free.
You can modify the workout to suit your fitness level.

en: complete control of trade in particular goods or of the supply of a particular service
examples: The company had a monopoly on internet services in the area.
No single supermarket should have a monopoly over food prices.

en: used to introduce an extra piece of information that supports what has already been said
examples: The restaurant is affordable; moreover, the food is excellent.
He's hardworking and, moreover, always willing to help others.

en: a legal agreement by which a bank lends you money to buy a house, and you pay the money back over a period of years
examples: They took out a 25-year mortgage to buy their first home.
Paying the mortgage is our biggest monthly expense.

en: a reason for doing something
examples: His main motive for changing jobs was to spend more time with his family.
What was the motive behind her sudden decision to move abroad?

en: felt or done equally by two or more people towards each other
examples: They became friends through a mutual love of hiking.
The decision to break up was mutual — they both agreed.

en: used to introduce more exact or detailed information about something
examples: There's one thing I really miss about home, namely my mum's cooking.
Two students passed with top marks, namely Sarah and Tom.

en: to find the right way to deal with a difficult situation; to find your way
examples: It can be hard to navigate a new city without a map app.
She helped me navigate the complicated visa application process.

en: not far away; close
examples: Is there a pharmacy nearby? I need to buy some medicine.
We parked the car and walked to a nearby restaurant for lunch.

en: to fail to take care of or give enough attention to someone or something
examples: Don't neglect your health just because you're busy with work.
The garden had been neglected for years and was full of weeds.

en: to try to reach an agreement by formal discussion
examples: She negotiated a better price for the car before buying it.
The two sides are still negotiating the terms of the contract.

en: despite what has just been said; nevertheless
examples: The weather was terrible; nonetheless, we enjoyed the trip.
It's a small restaurant but nonetheless very popular with locals.

en: a standard or pattern of behaviour that is typical or expected
examples: Working from home has become the norm for many people.
Social norms vary greatly from one culture to another.

en: important, interesting, or deserving attention
examples: There has been a notable increase in the number of cyclists in the city.
The most notable feature of the hotel was its rooftop swimming pool.

en: despite something; without being affected by something
examples: Notwithstanding the rain, we decided to go ahead with the picnic.
The team won the match, notwithstanding the loss of their best player.

en: to care for and encourage the growth or development of someone or something
examples: Parents play a key role in nurturing their children's confidence.
She nurtured her love of painting from a young age into a career.

en: to force someone to do something; to do something that someone has asked you to do
examples: The new law obliges restaurants to display calorie counts on menus.
I asked if she could swap shifts with me, and she kindly obliged.

en: a description of events, especially in a novel or story
examples: The movie has a compelling narrative that keeps you watching.
She gave a detailed narrative of her trip to Japan.

en: despite something that you have just mentioned
examples: The weather was terrible. Nevertheless, we had a great time.
It's a difficult exam; nevertheless, I think you should try.

en: having fine personal qualities that people admire, such as courage and honesty
examples: It was noble of him to volunteer at the shelter every weekend.
She comes from a noble family with a long history.

en: to formally suggest somebody for a job, a prize, or an election
examples: She was nominated for the best employee award this year.
The committee nominated him as the new chairperson.

en: used to draw attention to something important or to a particular example
examples: Several people helped, notably my neighbour who cooked meals for us.
The city has changed notably in the last ten years.

en: to formally or officially tell somebody about something
examples: Please notify us if you change your address.
The school notified parents about the schedule change.

zh: 臭名昭著的；声名狼藉的
en: well known for being bad or unpleasant
examples: That road is notorious for traffic jams during rush hour.
He's notorious for arriving late to every meeting.

en: different from anything known before; new, interesting, and often seeming slightly strange
examples: She came up with a novel way to organize her wardrobe.
That's a novel approach to solving the parking problem.

en: existing in large numbers
examples: I've been to that café on numerous occasions.
Numerous studies show that exercise improves mental health.

en: a reason why you disagree with or disapprove of something
examples: Does anyone have any objections to the new plan?
She raised an objection to the idea of working on weekends.

en: something that you must do because of a law, rule, or promise
examples: You have no obligation to buy anything after the free trial.
Parents have an obligation to look after their children.

en: not well known; difficult to understand
examples: He enjoys watching obscure foreign films that nobody has heard of.
The instructions were so obscure that no one could follow them.

en: to think about something or somebody too much and in a way that is not normal
examples: She tends to obsess over every small detail before a presentation.
Don't obsess about your weight — focus on being healthy.

en: something that makes it difficult for you to do something or go somewhere
examples: Lack of funding is the biggest obstacle to our project.
She overcame many obstacles to become a successful chef.

en: to get something, especially by making an effort
examples: You can obtain a visa at the embassy.
She obtained her driving licence on the first attempt.

en: to fill or use a space, area, or amount of time
examples: Books and photos occupy most of the shelf space in my room.
Cooking and cleaning occupy a lot of my time on weekends.

en: the degree to which something is likely to happen
examples: The odds of winning the lottery are extremely low.
Against all odds, he recovered fully from the injury.

en: to make somebody feel upset or annoyed because of something you say or do
examples: I'm sorry if my comment offended you — it wasn't intentional.
She was offended when he forgot her birthday.

en: continuing to exist or develop
examples: The renovation is an ongoing project that should finish by summer.
There's an ongoing debate about whether to change the school uniform.

en: that you can choose to do or have if you want to
examples: The guided tour is optional — you can explore on your own.
Wearing a tie to the office is optional on Fridays.

en: to happen or appear for the first time in a particular place or situation
examples: The tradition of afternoon tea originated in England.
The idea for the app originated from a conversation with friends.

en: used to state what the result would be if something did not happen
examples: You should leave now, otherwise you'll miss the train.
The food was a bit cold, but otherwise the dinner was lovely.

en: the sudden start of something unpleasant, especially a disease or violence
examples: There was an outbreak of food poisoning at the school cafeteria.
The outbreak of flu kept many workers at home last winter.

en: the result or effect of an action or event
examples: We're still waiting for the outcome of the job interview.
The outcome of the match surprised everyone.

en: to give the main facts or ideas about something
examples: She outlined her plans for redecorating the living room.
The teacher outlined what topics the exam would cover.

en: your attitude to life and the world; the likely future situation
examples: She has a very positive outlook on life despite the challenges.
The economic outlook for next year looks promising.

en: the amount of something that a person, machine, or organization produces
examples: The factory has increased its output by 20% this year.
She was praised for her creative output during the project.

en: a strong feeling of shock and anger
examples: There was public outrage over the increase in bus fares.
His rude behaviour caused outrage among the other guests.

en: extremely good; excellent
examples: She received an award for her outstanding performance at work.
The view from the hotel room was absolutely outstanding.

en: to succeed in dealing with or controlling a problem
examples: She overcame her fear of public speaking by practising regularly.
He had to overcome many difficulties to finish his degree.

en: to fail to notice or consider something; to have a view of something from above
examples: It's easy to overlook small mistakes when you're in a hurry.
Our balcony overlooks a beautiful garden.

en: in or to a foreign country, especially one across the sea
examples: She's been working overseas for the past three years.
Many students dream of studying overseas.

en: very great or very strong; so powerful that you cannot resist it
examples: Starting a new job can feel overwhelming at first.
The response to the charity event was overwhelming.

en: having skin that is light in colour, often because of illness or fear; light in colour
examples: You look pale — are you feeling all right?
She painted the bedroom walls a pale blue.

en: a situation or statement that seems impossible or contradictory but may be true
examples: It's a paradox that the busiest people often find the most time to help.
The paradox of choice is that too many options can make us less happy.

en: to some extent; not completely
examples: The delay was partly due to the bad weather.
I'm partly responsible for what happened.

en: an official document that identifies you as a citizen of a particular country and allows you to travel abroad
examples: Make sure your passport is valid before you book your flight.
I need to renew my passport — it expires next month.

en: the ability to stay calm and accept a delay or something annoying without complaining
examples: Learning a new language requires a lot of patience.
Thank you for your patience while we fix the issue.

en: the highest point or level of something
examples: Traffic reaches its peak between 8 and 9 in the morning.
She was at the peak of her career when she decided to retire.

en: strange or unusual, especially in a way that is unpleasant or worrying
examples: There's a peculiar smell coming from the fridge.
He has a peculiar habit of talking to himself while cooking.

en: a punishment for breaking a law, rule, or contract
examples: There's a penalty for returning library books late.
The company faced a heavy penalty for polluting the river.

en: to notice or become aware of something; to understand something in a particular way
examples: She perceived a change in his attitude after the argument.
The product is widely perceived as good value for money.

en: continuing to do something despite difficulties; lasting for a long time
examples: She was persistent in her search for a better job.
He has a persistent cough that won't go away.

en: a particular way of thinking about something; a point of view
examples: Travelling gave me a new perspective on life.
Try to see things from her perspective before judging.

en: a written document signed by many people that asks somebody in authority to do or change something
examples: Over 5,000 people signed the petition to save the local park.
They started an online petition against the new parking rules.

en: connected with making and selling drugs and medicines
examples: She works for a pharmaceutical company that develops vaccines.
The pharmaceutical industry invests heavily in research.

en: a fact or event in nature or society, especially one that is not fully understood
examples: Social media has become a global phenomenon.
The northern lights are a spectacular natural phenomenon.

en: a person who is the first to study, develop, or do something new
examples: She was a pioneer in the field of renewable energy.
The pioneers of the internet changed how we communicate.

en: to formally promise to give or do something
examples: He pledged to donate 10% of his salary to charity.
The company pledged to reduce its carbon emissions by 2030.

en: to fall or move suddenly and quickly downward; to decrease suddenly
examples: Temperatures plunged below zero last night.
She plunged into the swimming pool to cool off.

en: to describe or show somebody/something in a particular way; to act a part in a film or play
examples: The film portrays life in a small village during the 1950s.
She was portrayed as a villain, but she's actually very kind.

en: to create a threat, problem, etc.; to sit or stand in a particular position for a photograph
examples: Climate change poses a serious threat to coastal cities.
Everyone posed for a group photo at the wedding.

en: the state of having or owning something; a thing that you own
examples: The house came into her possession after her grandmother passed away.
He packed all his possessions into two suitcases.

en: to arrange for something to take place at a later time or date
examples: We had to postpone the picnic because of the rain.
The meeting has been postponed until next Monday.

en: that can develop into something or be developed in the future
examples: We need to be aware of potential risks before starting the project.
She's a potential candidate for the manager position.

en: a person who works in a profession, especially medicine or law
examples: You should consult a medical practitioner if the pain continues.
She's a skilled practitioner of traditional Chinese medicine.

en: something that is done in advance in order to prevent problems or to avoid danger
examples: Take the precaution of wearing sunscreen, even on cloudy days.
As a precaution, the school cancelled outdoor activities during the storm.

en: exactly; in an accurate and careful way
examples: The train arrived at precisely 9:15, just as scheduled.
That's precisely what I was trying to say.

en: happening or behaving in a way that you would expect and therefore not interesting
examples: The ending of the movie was too predictable.
His reaction was entirely predictable — he always gets upset about these things.

en: mostly; mainly
examples: The audience was predominantly made up of young people.
The neighbourhood is predominantly residential with a few shops.

en: an unreasonable dislike of or preference for a person, group, or thing, especially when it is not based on reason
examples: We should fight against prejudice in all its forms.
She faced prejudice because of her accent when she moved to the city.

en: happening before a more important action or event, especially in order to prepare for it
examples: We need to do some preliminary research before writing the report.
The preliminary results of the survey look very promising.

en: a statement or idea that forms the basis for an argument or theory
examples: The premise of the book is that happiness comes from within.
His argument is based on a false premise.

en: to tell somebody to take a particular medicine or have a particular treatment; to say what should be done
examples: The doctor prescribed some antibiotics for the infection.
The rules prescribe that all employees must wear ID badges.

en: respected and admired as very important or of very high quality
examples: She graduated from a prestigious university in London.
He won a prestigious award for his photography.

en: used to say that you think something is probably true
examples: Presumably, he'll call us when he arrives at the airport.
She's not here, so presumably she's still at work.

en: to exist or be very common at a particular time or in a particular place; to be accepted
examples: A positive atmosphere prevails in the office since the new manager arrived.
Common sense prevailed and they decided to cancel the outdoor event.

en: that exists or is very common at a particular time or in a particular place
examples: Allergies are becoming more prevalent among children.

en: one of several parts of which something is made
examples: Exercise is a key component of a healthy lifestyle.
The factory makes electronic components for computers.

en: to have something as parts or members; to be those parts or members
examples: The committee comprises ten members from different departments.
Women comprise about 45 percent of the total workforce.

en: something that you agree to do or give to somebody in order to end an argument
examples: Both sides had to make concessions to reach an agreement.
The government made no concessions to the protesters.

en: the quality of always behaving or performing in a similar way
examples: Consistency is the key to improving your language skills.
There was a lack of consistency in the way the rules were applied.

en: a secret plan by a group of people to do something illegal or harmful
examples: He was found guilty of conspiracy to commit fraud.
Many people believe conspiracy theories about the event.

en: a meeting with an expert to get advice; a discussion before a decision is made
examples: The doctor offers a free consultation for new patients.
The decision was made after consultation with all team members.

en: to compete against somebody to gain something; to say that something is true
examples: Three candidates are contending for the leadership position.
She contends that the new policy is unfair to workers.

en: causing a lot of angry public discussion and disagreement
examples: The decision to close the school was highly controversial.
The politician made several controversial statements during the debate.

en: to arrange for people to come together for a formal meeting
examples: The president convened an emergency meeting of the cabinet.
The committee will convene again next Monday.

en: the process of changing something from one form, use, or system to another
examples: The conversion of the old factory into apartments took two years.
The app handles currency conversion automatically.

en: the act of organizing people or things so that they work together effectively
examples: The project requires close coordination between all departments.
Good hand-eye coordination is important for playing sports.
